architecture.summary: The system injects explicit verb knowledge from curated lexical resources (VerbNet, FrameNet) into pretrained Transformer encoders via dedicated adapter modules. Verb adapters are trained on a binary word-pair classification task (predicting if two verbs belong to the same semantic class) using lexical constraints extracted from the resources. During downstream fine-tuning for event extraction, the verb adapters complement the pretrained LM knowledge either through full fine-tuning (both Transformer and adapter parameters updated) or task-adapter fine-tuning (Transformer and verb adapters frozen, new task adapter trained on top). For cross-lingual transfer, the approach either applies English-trained adapters zero-shot via multilingual BERT, or automatically translates English verb pairs to target languages, filters them via a relation prediction model, and trains target-language adapters.

biblio.title: Verb Knowledge Injection for Multilingual Event Processing
biblio.venue: Proceedings of ACL-IJCNLP 2021 Volume 1 Long Papers 6952-6969

claims.statement: VerbNet adapter injection yields consistent statistically significant improvements on English TempEval event trigger identification and classification in task-adapter setup
FrameNet adapter provides significant performance boosts on English ACE trigger identification task
VerbNet adapter improves English ACE argument identification task performance in task-adapter setup
FrameNet adapter improves zero-shot transfer on Spanish TempEval via multilingual BERT in task-adapter setup
Noisily translated VerbNet verb pairs via vtrans yield consistent gains on Chinese TempEval in monolingual task-adapter setup
FrameNet adapter provides significant improvements on Arabic ACE trigger identification via zero-shot mBERT transfer

Random adapter baseline (same architecture as verb adapter but randomly initialized) shows no consistent improvements, indicating gains stem from the verb knowledge signal rather than parameter capacity alone

claims.dataset: Spanish, Chinese, Arabic TempEval and ACE
claims.statement: Noisily translated verb pairs carry sufficient useful signal through to target languages despite translation errors from imperfect cross-lingual embedding spaces and polysemy
